blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلسَّمَآءِ ذَاتِ ٱلْبُرُوجِ (١)
By the heaven with its Houses, (1)
وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْمَوْعُودِ (٢)
by the Promised Day, (2)
وَشَاهِدٍ وَمَشْهُودٍ (٣)
by the Witness and the Witnessed: (3)
قُتِلَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْأُخْدُودِ (٤)
perish the People of the Ditch! (4)
ٱلنَّارِ ذَاتِ ٱلْوَقُودِ (٥)
The fire abounding in fuel, (5)
إِذْ هُمْ عَلَيْهَا قُعُودٌ (٦)
above which they sat (6)
وَهُمْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَفْعَلُونَ بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ شُهُودٌ (٧)
as they were themselves witnesses to what they did to the faithful. (7)
وَمَا نَقَمُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ إِلَّآ أَن يُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَمِيدِ (٨)
They were vindictive towards them only because they had faith in Allah, the All-mighty and the All-laudable, (8)
ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدٌ (٩)
to whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and Allah is witness to all things. (9)

Content of the Surah and Its Virtue

The believers in Mecca, especially at the beginning of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) mission, were in severe distress and under immense pressure. They were constantly subjected to mental and physical torture by the enemies to force them to renounce their faith. A group resisted, while some weaker individuals surrendered and turned back.

Given that this Surah is among the Meccan Surahs, it appears that its primary objective is to strengthen the morale of the believers against this current and to encourage them toward steadfastness and perseverance.

In this regard, the Surah recounts the story of the "People of the Ditch" (Ashab al-Ukhdud)—those who dug trenches and kindled great fires within them, threatening the believers with torture by fire. They burned a group of them alive, yet they did not turn back from their faith.

In another part of this Surah, Allah severely attacks the disbelievers who put the believers under pressure and threatens them with the burning punishment of Hell, while giving glad tidings to the believers of gardens full of heavenly blessings.

In the subsequent section, He takes them back through history, visualizing before their eyes the story of Pharaoh, Thamud, and the powerful, rebellious nations—how they were brought to their knees and destroyed before the command of Divine punishment. This served as a warning to the disbelievers of Mecca, whose power was insignificant compared to them, and as a source of consolation for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the believers.

In the final section of the Surah, He points to the greatness of the Glorious Quran and the extraordinary importance of this Divine revelation, concluding the Surah with it.

Altogether, this is a Surah of perseverance, steadfastness, and patience against the pressures exerted by oppressors and the arrogant upon the believers, and within its verses lies the hidden promise of Divine victory.

The naming of this Surah as "Al-Buruj" is due to the oath mentioned in the first verse.

Regarding the virtue of this Surah, it is sufficient to mention a Hadith from the Noble Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله):

من قرأ هذه السورة اعطاه اللَّه من الاجر بعدد كل من اجتمع فى جمعة و كل من اجتمع يوم عرفه عشر حسنات، و قراءتها تنجى من المخاوف و الشدائد:

"Whoever recites this Surah, Allah will grant him ten rewards for every person who gathers for Friday prayer and every person who gathers on the Day of Arafah (in Arafat); and its recitation delivers one from fears and hardships." [1] Considering that one of the interpretations of the verse "وَ شاهِدٍ وَ مَشْهُودٍ" is the Day of Friday and the Day of Arafah, and also considering that the Surah narrates the intense resistance of past believers against hardships and pressures, the correlation of these rewards with the content of the Surah becomes clear. Furthermore, it shows that all this reward and recompense is for those who recite it, reflect upon it, and then act accordingly.

[1]: "Tafsir al-Burhan," Vol. 4, p. 445.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

وَ السَّماءِ ذاتِ الْبُرُوجِ (1) وَ الْيَوْمِ الْمَوْعُودِ (2) وَ شاهِدٍ وَ مَشْهُودٍ (3) قُتِلَ أَصْحابُ الْأُخْدُودِ (4)

النَّارِ ذاتِ الْوَقُودِ (5) إِذْ هُمْ عَلَيْها قُعُودٌ (6) وَ هُمْ عَلى‌ ما يَفْعَلُونَ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ شُهُودٌ (7) وَ ما نَقَمُوا مِنْهُمْ إِلاَّ أَنْ يُؤْمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَمِيدِ (8) الَّذِي لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ شَهِيدٌ (9)

Commentary

Believers Before the Human-Burning Furnaces!

We know that the Muslims in Mecca were initially under severe pressure, and the enemies inflicted every kind of torture upon them. As we mentioned in the description of the Surah's content, the purpose of revealing this Surah is to warn these torturers to remember the fate of similar nations in history. On the other hand, it is a consolation, comfort, and morale boost for the early believers and a lesson for all Muslims throughout history.

First, He says: "By the heaven containing great stars (constellations)" (وَ السَّماءِ ذاتِ الْبُرُوجِ‌).

"Buruj" is the plural of "Burj," which originally means a palace. Some consider it to mean "something apparent and manifest," and they believe that high palaces and tall buildings were named this because of their clarity and visibility. For this reason, a specific part of a city wall or a military gathering place that has a distinct visibility is called a "Burj." When a woman displays her adornments, they say "tabarrajat al-mar'ah" [1]. The celestial "Buruj" either refers to the bright and shining stars of the sky or to the "constellations"—that is, a group of stars that appear to us to resemble an earthly creature. The twelve signs of the zodiac are twelve constellations that the sun aligns with one of them each month in its annual path (of course, the sun does not move; rather, the earth revolves around it, but it appears as though the sun moves and aligns with one of these constellations) [2]. Whichever of these meanings the oath refers to, it signifies its greatness—a greatness that perhaps was not clear to the Arabs at that time but is fully recognized by us today. However, it seems more likely that the intended meaning is the bright celestial stars.

[1]: Some researchers believe this word is derived from the Persian word "Barz," meaning height, greatness, and glory (refer to Borhan-e Qate' and its footnotes).

[2]: These twelve signs are Aries, Taurus, Gemini, Cancer, Leo, Virgo, Libra, Scorpio, Sagittarius, Capricorn, Aquarius, and Pisces.

Therefore, it is mentioned in a Hadith that the Noble Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was asked for the interpretation of this verse, and He said: "It refers to the planets and stars." [1]

Then He adds: "And by the Promised Day" (the Day of Resurrection) (وَ الْيَوْمِ الْمَوْعُودِ). The very day that all the Prophets and Divine Messengers have promised, and hundreds of verses of the Glorious Quran report of it; the day that is the appointment and meeting place for all the first and the last, and the day when everyone's accounts must be settled.

In the third and fourth oaths, He says: "And by the witness and what is witnessed" (وَ شاهِدٍ وَ مَشْهُودٍ). Regarding what is meant by "witness" (Shahid) and "witnessed" (Mashhud), numerous interpretations have been mentioned, reaching over thirty. The most important of them are the following:

1- The "witness" is the person of the Noble Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), as the Quran says:

يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ إِنَّا أَرْسَلْناكَ شاهِداً وَ مُبَشِّراً وَ نَذِيراً: "O Prophet, indeed We have sent you as a witness and a bringer of good tidings and a warner" (Al-Ahzab: 45).

And the "witnessed" is the Day of Resurrection itself, as the Quran says: ذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَجْمُوعٌ لَهُ النَّاسُ وَ ذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَشْهُودٌ: "That is a Day for which the people will be collected, and that is a Day [that will be] witnessed" (Hud: 103).

2- The "witness" refers to the witnesses of human actions, such as the limbs of his body, as we read in verse 24 of Surah An-Nur: يَوْمَ تَشْهَدُ عَلَيْهِمْ أَلْسِنَتُهُمْ وَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ أَرْجُلُهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ‌: "On a Day when their tongues, their hands and their feet will bear witness against them as to what they used to do."

And the "witnessed" are the human beings and their deeds.

3- "Witness" means Friday, which witnesses the gathering of Muslims in the very important ceremony of that day's prayer.

And the "witnessed" is the Day of Arafah, which the pilgrims of the House of Allah witness and observe. This interpretation has been narrated in a tradition from the Noble Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام), and Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام). [1]

4- "Witness" is the Day of Eid al-Adha and "witnessed" is the Day of Arafah (the day before it).

It is mentioned in a Hadith that "a man entered the Mosque of the Noble Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and saw someone sitting and narrating Hadith from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله). He said: I asked him for the interpretation of this verse. He said: Yes, 'witness' is Friday and 'witnessed' is the Day of Arafah. I passed him and reached another who was speaking of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله). I asked him for the interpretation of this same verse. He said: 'Witness' is Friday and 'witnessed' is the Day of Eid al-Adha. I passed him too and reached a handsome youth who was also narrating Hadith from the Messenger of Allah. I said: Tell me about the interpretation of this verse. He said: 'Witness' is Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) and 'witnessed' is the Day of Resurrection. Have you not heard that Allah says:

'يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ إِنَّا أَرْسَلْناكَ شاهِداً وَ مُبَشِّراً وَ نَذِيراً' and have you not heard Him say: 'ذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَجْمُوعٌ لَهُ النَّاسُ وَ ذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَشْهُودٌ'?

I asked who the first person was; they said: Ibn Abbas. I asked about the second person; they said: Abdullah ibn Umar. And I asked about the third person; they said: He is Hasan ibn Ali (عليه السلام)!" [1]

5- The intended meaning of "witness" is the nights and days, and "witnessed" is the children of Adam, whose deeds they testify to, just as we read in the supplication of Imam Zayn al-Abidin (عليه السلام) for morning and evening: هذا يوم حادث جديد و هو علينا شاهد عتيد، ان احسنا ودعنا بحمد، و ان اسانا فارقنا بذنب: "This is a new day, and it is a witness ready over us. If we do good, it bids us farewell with praise, and if we do evil, it departs from us with blame." [2]

6- The "witness" refers to the angels and the "witnessed" is the Quran.

7- The "witness" is the Black Stone (Hajar al-Aswad) and the "witnessed" are the pilgrims who come to it and place their hands upon it.

8- "Witness" is the creation and "witnessed" is the Truth (Allah).

9- The "witness" is the Islamic Ummah and the "witnessed" are other nations, as stated in verse 143 of Surah Al-Baqarah: لِتَكُونُوا شُهَداءَ عَلَى النَّاسِ‌ "That you may be witnesses over the people."

10- "Witness" is the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) and "witnessed" are the other Prophets, as evidenced by verse 41 of Surah An-Nisa: وَ جِئْنا بِكَ عَلى‌ هؤُلاءِ شَهِيداً: "And We bring you [O Muhammad] as a witness against these [other Prophets]."

11- Or that "witness" is the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and "witnessed" is the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام).

Of course, the harmony of this verse with the preceding verses necessitates that it refers to the witnesses of the Day of Resurrection, whether it be the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) or other Prophets regarding their nations, the angels, the limbs of the human body, the night and day, and the like; and the "witnessed" are the humans or their deeds.

In this way, many of the above interpretations merge and are summarized into one collection with a broad concept.

However, interpretations such as "Friday," the "Day of Arafah," and the "Day of Eid" are distinct from this meaning, although they too are among the witnesses of the Day of Gathering and the witnesses of human deeds; rather, each of these highly attended days is considered a scene of the Resurrection in this world.

Given this explanation, it becomes clear that there is no contradiction between the above interpretations, and it is possible for the broad concept of "witness" and "witnessed" to encompass them all. This is one of the signs of the Quran's greatness—that it possesses concepts so vast they accommodate many interpretations. For "witness" includes every kind of testifier, and "witnessed" includes everything that is testified upon. The fact that both are mentioned in the "indefinite" (nakirah) form points to the greatness of this witness and witnessed, which is well-reflected in all the above interpretations.

A noteworthy point is that there is a subtle link between these four oaths on one hand, and the matter for which the oath was taken on the other. The sky, the bright stars, and its balanced constellations are all signs of order and accounting. The "Promised Day" is a clear scene of accounting and reckoning. The witness and witnessed are also means for the precise investigation of this account. Furthermore, all these oaths are meant to warn the oppressive torturers that their actions against the true believers are all recorded and preserved for the Promised Day, and the witnesses that surround your existence—including the angels, the limbs of the body, the nights and days, and the like—are monitoring all these acts and will testify on that day. [1]

Therefore, after these oaths, He says: "Cursed be the companions of the trench!" (قُتِلَ أَصْحابُ الْأُخْدُودِ).

"The fire full of fuel," (النَّارِ ذاتِ الْوَقُودِ).

"When they were sitting by it," (إِذْ هُمْ عَلَيْها قُعُودٌ).

"And they were, to what they were doing against the believers, witnesses." (وَ هُمْ عَلى‌ ما يَفْعَلُونَ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ شُهُودٌ).

"Ukhdud," according to Raghib in "Al-Mufradat," means a wide, deep, and extensive split in the ground, or in other words, large pits and trenches. Its plural is "Akhadid." Originally, it is derived from the human "Khadd," which refers to the two depressions on either side of the human nose on the right and left (where tears flow). It was then metaphorically applied to a pit that appears on the face of the earth (and later became a literal meaning).

As for who this group of torturers were and in what time they lived, commentators and historians have various opinions, the details of which will come, God willing, in the points following the verses. However, what is certain is that they had prepared large trenches of fire and were forcing the believers to renounce their faith. When they met with their resistance, they threw them into these human-burning furnaces and set them ablaze! "Waqud" originally means the material with which a fire is kindled (like firewood). The expression "dhat al-waqud" (possessing fuel), despite the fact that all fires need wood and the like, points to the abundance of flammable materials they used, which naturally created a very extensive and flaming fire. If some have interpreted "ذاتِ الْوَقُودِ" here as "flaming fire," it is apparently for this reason, not what some have imagined—that "waqud" has two meanings, one being firewood and the other "flame"—and expressed regret that commentators and translators have not noticed this point!

The meaning of the verse "إِذْ هُمْ عَلَيْها قُعُودٌ" and the following verse is that a group sat with complete cold-bloodedness and watched the scenes of torture and took pleasure in it, which itself was a sign of their ultimate cruelty.

Some have also said that this group was tasked with interrogation and forcing the believers to leave the true religion.

Some have also considered them to be two groups: one group of "torturers" and one group of "spectators." Since the spectators were pleased with the actions of the torturers, this action was attributed to all of them. It is natural that in such a program, there is always a group of actors and a group of spectators; moreover, their leaders usually give the orders and the agents are from the lower classes.

It has also been said that a group sat and monitored the torture agents so they would not deviate from their program, and to testify before the king that they performed their duties well! The composition of that crowd from all these different groups does not seem unlikely either; therefore, combining all these interpretations is also possible.

In any case, the phrase "yaf'alun" (they were doing) in the present continuous form is evidence that this action continued for some time and was not a fleeting incident.

Then He adds: "And they resented them not except because they believed in Allah, the Exalted in Might, the Praiseworthy," (وَ ما نَقَمُوا مِنْهُمْ إِلَّا أَنْ يُؤْمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَمِيدِ). Yes, their only crime and sin was faith in the One and Only God—the Powerful God who is worthy of all praise and encompasses every perfection. Is faith in such a God a sin? Or faith in idols devoid of intellect and sense and empty of any merit? "Naqamu" is from the root "naqam" (rhyming with qalam), meaning to deny and find fault with something, either by tongue or practically through punishment; "intiqam" (revenge) is also from this root.

Surely, such an act is done in response to a great crime and an obvious sin, not in response to faith in the Almighty God who is worthy of every praise. This shows to what extent the culture of that people was decadent and distorted, such that the greatest of honors was considered the greatest crime and sin in their eyes.

In any case, this is similar to what is mentioned in verse 59 of Surah Al-Ma'idah, where the sorcerers, after believing in Moses and being threatened with torture and death by Pharaoh, said to him: وَ ما تَنْقِمُ مِنَّا إِلَّا أَنْ آمَنَّا بِآياتِ رَبِّنا: "And you do not resent us except because we believed in the signs of our Lord."

The use of "Aziz" (the Invincible Power) and "Hamid" (Worthy of all praise and possessing every perfection) is in fact a response to their crimes and an argument against them—meaning, is faith in such a God a crime and a sin?! At the same time, it is considered a threat and a warning to these torturers throughout history that the Almighty and Praiseworthy God is lying in wait for them.

Then, describing two other attributes of this great Object of Worship, He adds: "The One to whom belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth. And Allah, over all things, is Witness." (الَّذِي لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ شَهِيدٌ). In fact, these four attributes are among those that make the worthiness for worship certain: power and ability, possessing every perfection, ownership of the heavens and the earth, and awareness of everything.

At the same time, it is a glad tiding to the believers that God is present and observing, and He sees their patience, endurance, and steadfastness in the path of preserving faith, and He witnesses their altruism and sacrifices. Certainly, awareness of this reality gives them strength and joy.

On the other hand, it is a threat to their enemies and a warning that if God does not prevent their actions, it is not due to inability, but for the sake of trial and testing, and eventually, they will taste the bitter flavor of God's painful punishment.

Key Points

1- Who Were the People of the Ditch?

We said "Ukhdud" means a large pit or "trench," and what is meant here are the massive trenches that were filled with fire so that the torturers could throw the believers into them and burn them.

Regarding what time and what people this event pertains to, and whether it was one specific and certain event or an allusion to multiple such events in different regions of the world, there is discussion among commentators and historians.

The most famous view is that it pertains to "Dhu Nuwas," the last king of the "Himyar" [1] in the land of "Yemen."

To explain: "Dhu Nuwas," who was the last of the Himyar dynasty, converted to Judaism, and the Himyar group followed him. He took the name "Yusuf," and some time passed in this manner. Then he was informed that in the land of "Najran" (in northern Yemen), there was still a group following the religion of Christianity. Dhu Nuwas's co-religionists urged him to force the people of Najran to accept Judaism. He marched toward Najran, gathered its inhabitants, presented the Jewish faith to them, and insisted they accept it. But they refused; they were ready to accept martyrdom but were not ready to renounce their faith.

"Dhu Nuwas" ordered a massive trench to be dug, filled it with wood, and set it on fire. He burned a group of them alive and killed another group with the sword, cutting them to pieces, such that the number of those killed and burned reached twenty thousand people! [1]

Some have added that during this turmoil, one man from the Christians of Najran escaped and fled toward Rome to the court of Caesar, complaining against Dhu Nuwas and seeking help.

"Caesar" said: "Your land is far from me, but I will write a letter to the King of Abyssinia (Ethiopia), for he is a Christian and your neighbor, and I will ask him to help you." Then he wrote a letter seeking revenge for the blood of the Christians of Najran. The man from Najran came to the King of Abyssinia, the Negus (Najashi). The Negus was deeply moved by hearing this story and grieved over the extinguishing of the flame of the religion of Christ (عليه السلام) in the land of Najran, and he decided to take revenge for the martyrs.

The Abyssinian armies marched toward Yemen and, in a fierce battle, defeated the army of Dhu Nuwas. A large number of them were killed, and it was not long before the kingdom of Yemen fell into the hands of the Negus and became a province of Abyssinia. [2]

Some commentators have narrated that the length of that trench was forty cubits and its width was twelve cubits (a cubit is approximately half a meter, and sometimes used to mean a "yard" which is about one meter). Some have narrated there were seven pits, each with the dimensions mentioned above. [1]

The above event has appeared in various forms in many books of commentary and history, including by the great commentator "Tabarsi" in "Majma' al-Bayan," "Abu al-Futuh Razi" in his commentary, "Fakhr Razi" in the Great Commentary (Tafsir al-Kabir), "Alusi" in "Ruh al-Ma'ani," and "Qurtubi" in his commentary under the verses in question, as well as "Ibn Hisham" in his Sira (Vol. 1, p. 35) and many others.

From what we have said above, it becomes clear that these heartless torturers eventually met with Divine punishment, and revenge for the blood they had shed was taken from them in this very world, and the punishment of the burning fire of Resurrection also awaits them.

These human-burning furnaces created by the Jews were likely the first human-burning furnaces in history. But the strange thing is that this cruel, anti-human innovation eventually caught up with the Jews themselves; as we know, a large group of them were burned in human-burning furnaces during the events of Hitler's Germany, and the manifestation of the "punishment of the burning fire" of this world was also realized regarding them.

Furthermore, "Dhu Nuwas the Jew," the original founder of this sinister structure, did not remain safe from the evil of his own deeds.

What was said above regarding the People of the Ditch is according to the well-known and famous theory. However, there are other narrations in this regard showing that the People of the Ditch were not only in "Yemen" and in the era of "Dhu Nuwas," to the extent that some commentators have cited ten opinions regarding the People of the Ditch!

In a narration from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), we read that he said: "The Magi (Zoroastrians) were People of the Book and acted according to their Divine Book. One of their kings slept with his sister, and that woman asked the king to make marriage with a sister permissible. But the people did not accept and did not submit. The king threw a group of believers who did not accept his word into a trench of fire." [1] This pertains to the "People of the Ditch of Persia." Regarding the "People of the Ditch of Syria," they have also written that a believing people lived there, and "Antiochus" threw them into a trench of fire. [2]

Some have also considered this event to pertain to the companions of "Daniel," the famous Prophet of the Children of Israel, and his companions, who are mentioned in the Book of Daniel in the Torah, and "Thalabi" has also applied the Ditch of Persia to them. [3]

It is not at all unlikely that "People of the Ditch" includes all of these and their like, although its most famous and well-known instance is the story of Dhu Nuwas in the land of Yemen.

2- Steadfastness in the Path of Preserving Faith

Sacrifice in preserving faith has had magnificent manifestations in the past and present. History shows many individuals who passionately drank the cup of affliction in this path, kissed the gallows or the edge of the tyrant's sword, and like moths, burned in the fire of the torturers—the names and signs of a group of whom are recorded in history.

We have all heard the story of Pharaoh's wife, "Asiya," who was subjected to all that torture for the sake of faith in "Moses son of Amram" and eventually lost her life in this path.

In a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), it is mentioned that Allah sent a Prophet from among the people of "Abyssinia" to them. They rose up to deny him, and a war broke out among them. Eventually, they killed a group of that Prophet's companions and took another group captive along with the Prophet himself. Then they prepared a place and filled it with fire, and invited the people to it, saying: "Whoever is upon our religion, let him step aside, and whoever holds the religion of this group must throw himself into the fire." The companions of that Prophet (seeing there was no other way, bravely threw themselves into the fire) and raced against each other. At that moment, a woman came who had a one-month-old infant in her arms. When she wanted to throw herself into the fire, maternal affection was stirred and she hesitated. The nursing infant cried out:

"My mother, do not fear! Throw both yourself and me! By Allah, this is a small thing in the way of Allah: ان هذا و اللَّه فى اللَّه قليل..." and this child was among those who spoke in the cradle. [1] From this story, it is understood that a fourth group of the People of the Ditch were in Abyssinia.

The story of the parents of Ammar ibn Yasir and other individuals like him, and even greater than that, the story of the companions of Imam Husayn (عليه السلام) in the field of sacrifice at "Karbala" and their racing against each other to drink the nectar of martyrdom, is famous in the history of Islam.

In our era as well, we have seen or heard of many examples of this subject with our own eyes, where young and old have placed their lives in their hands in the path of preserving religion and faith, stepping consciously and passionately toward martyrdom. It must be said: the survival of Divine religions in the past and today has not been and is not possible without such sacrifices.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ فَتَنُوا۟ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَتُوبُوا۟ فَلَهُمْ عَذَابُ جَهَنَّمَ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابُ ٱلْحَرِيقِ (١٠)
Indeed those who persecute the faithful, men and women, and do not repent thereafter, there is the punishment of hell for them and the punishment of burning. (10)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٌ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْكَبِيرُ (١١)
Indeed those who have faith and do righteous deeds—for them will be gardens with streams running in them. That is the supreme success. (11)
إِنَّ بَطْشَ رَبِّكَ لَشَدِيدٌ (١٢)
Indeed your Lord’s striking is severe. (12)
إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ يُبْدِئُ وَيُعِيدُ (١٣)
It is indeed He who originates and brings back, (13)
وَهُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلْوَدُودُ (١٤)
and He is the All-forgiving, the All-affectionate, (14)
ذُو ٱلْعَرْشِ ٱلْمَجِيدُ (١٥)
Lord of the Throne, the All-glorious, (15)
فَعَّالٌ لِّمَا يُرِيدُ (١٦)
doer of what He desires. (16)

Commentary

The Torturers Facing Divine Punishment

After describing the immense crime of the torturers of past nations—who burned steadfast believers alive in fire—these verses point to the severe Divine retribution awaiting those torturers and the magnificent rewards for the believers.

First, He says: "Indeed, those who have tortured the believing men and believing women and then have not repented will have the punishment of Hell, and they will have the punishment of the Burning Fire" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ فَتَنُوا الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَ الْمُؤْمِناتِ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَتُوبُوا فَلَهُمْ عَذابُ جَهَنَّمَ وَ لَهُمْ عَذابُ الْحَرِيقِ).

"Fatanu" (فَتَنُوا) is derived from the root "fatn" (فَتْن). "Fitnah" originally means placing gold in fire to determine its purity. Subsequently, this root has been used to mean "trial/testing," "punishment/retribution," and "misguidance/polytheism." In the verse under discussion, it means punishment, harassment, and torture. A similar usage appears in verses 13 and 14 of Surah Adh-Dhariyat: يَوْمَ هُمْ عَلَى النَّارِ يُفْتَنُونَ ذُوقُوا فِتْنَتَكُمْ هذَا الَّذِي كُنْتُمْ بِهِ تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ: "The Day they will be tormented over the Fire. [It will be said], 'Taste your torment! This is what you were impatient for!'"

The phrase "then have not repented" (ثُمَّ لَمْ يَتُوبُوا) shows that the path of repentance is open even for such tyrannical torturers. This demonstrates the ultimate Grace of the Lord toward sinners and, meanwhile, serves as a warning to the people of Makkah to cease their harassment and torture of the believers before it is too late, and to return to Allah.

In principle, the Quran never closes the door of return to anyone. This indicates that even the establishment of severe and painful punishments is for the reformation of the corrupt and the spreaders of corruption, intended to bring them back toward the Truth.

It is noteworthy that in this verse, two types of punishment are mentioned for them: one is the "punishment of Hell" and the other is the "punishment of the Burning Fire" (عذاب الحریق). The mention of these two might be because Hell contains various types of punishments, one of which is burning fire. Its specific mention is because the aforementioned torturers burned the believers with fire; thus, they must be punished with fire there—but what a difference between that fire and this one!

That fire is kindled by the flames of Allah's wrath and anger; it is an eternal and everlasting fire, a fire accompanied by humiliation and disgrace. However, the fire of this world is a transient fire kindled by weak creatures, and the believers who burn in it are honorable and proud, holding a place in the first rank of the martyrs in the way of Allah.

Some have also said that the "punishment of Hell" is for their disbelief (kufr), while the "punishment of the Burning Fire" is for their acts of torture.

Then, addressing the reward of the believers, He says: "Indeed, those who have believed and done righteous deeds will have gardens beneath which rivers flow. That is the great attainment" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَهُمْ جَنَّاتٌ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ ذلِكَ الْفَوْزُ الْكَبِيرُ).

What success and victory could be greater than being situated in the proximity of the Lord amidst various everlasting blessings, with honor and pride? However, it must not be forgotten that the primary key to this victory and "great attainment" is "faith and righteous deeds." This is the fundamental capital for this path; everything else is merely branches and leaves.

The expression "done righteous deeds" (عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ)—considering that salihat is the plural of salih—indicates that one or a few righteous acts are not enough; rather, the entire life program must revolve around righteous action.

The use of the word "dhalika" (ذلِكَ), which in the Arabic language is a demonstrative pronoun for the distant, signifies in such contexts the importance and loftiness of the status. That is, their victory, salvation, and honors are so great that they are beyond the reach of our immediate thought.

Then, once again turning to threaten the disbelievers and torturers, He adds: "Indeed, the forceful grip and punishment of your Lord is very severe!" (إِنَّ بَطْشَ رَبِّكَ لَشَدِيدٌ).

Next, He says: do not imagine that there is no Resurrection or that your return is difficult; "Indeed, it is He who originates creation and repeats it" (إِنَّهُ هُوَ يُبْدِئُ وَ يُعِيدُ). "Batsh" (بَطْش) means seizing with force and power, and since this act is often a prelude to punishment, the word has also come to mean retribution and penalty.

The expression "your Lord" (رَبِّكَ) is to comfort the heart of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and to emphasize Allah's support for him.

It is noteworthy that the above verse is accompanied by various forms of emphasis: first, the word "batsh" itself implies a forceful seizure and contains inherent severity; second, the "nominal sentence" (jumlah ismiyyah) usually denotes emphasis; third, the description "severe" (shadid); fourth, the word "inna" (إِنَّ); and fifth, the "lam" (لَـ) which is used for emphasis in such cases—all are gathered in this one verse.

Therefore, the Magnificent Quran intends to threaten them with punishment with the utmost decisiveness. The sentence "Indeed, it is He who originates and repeats," which contains the summarized proof of the Resurrection, adds another layer of emphasis. [^1]

Then, He proceeds to mention five of the attributes of the Great God, saying: "And He is the Forgiving of repentant servants, and the Loving of the believers" (وَ هُوَ الْغَفُورُ الْوَدُودُ).

"He is the Owner of the Throne of power, and absolute sovereignty over the universe, and the Possessor of glory and majesty" (ذُو الْعَرْشِ الْمَجِيدُ).

"He is the Doer of whatever He intends" (فَعَّالٌ لِما يُرِيدُ). "Ghafur" (آمرزنده) and "Wadud" (دوستدار), both being intensive forms (sighah mubalaghah), point to the ultimate extent of His forgiveness

[^1]: This beautiful proof regarding the "Resurrection" is the same as what appears at the end of Surah Ya-Sin, verse 79: قُلْ يُحْيِيهَا الَّذِي أَنْشَأَها أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَ هُوَ بِكُلِّ خَلْقٍ عَلِيمٌ: "Say, 'He will give them life who produced them the first time; and He is, of all creation, Knowing.'" It is said that Al-Farabi wished that Aristotle, the famous Greek philosopher, were alive to hear this firm and beautiful proof regarding the Resurrection from the Magnificent Quran!

and His love: Forgiving and Pardoning toward repentant sinners, and full of love toward righteous servants.

In reality, mentioning these attributes alongside the threat in the preceding verses is to express the truth that the path of return is open to sinners, and Allah, while being severe in punishment, is Forgiving, Loving, Compassionate, and Merciful.

From what has been said, it becomes clear that "Wadud" here carries an active meaning (the One who loves) rather than a passive one. Some who considered "Wadud" to be a "passive participle" (ism maf'ul)—like "rakub" meaning "markub" (that which is ridden)—implying that Allah is "greatly loved," does not harmonize with the attribute "Ghafur" that precedes it. This is because the goal is to express the Lord's love toward His servants, not the servants' affection and love toward Him.

The third attribute, "Owner of the Throne" (ذُو الْعَرْشِ), considering that the Throne (Arsh)—meaning a high royal seat—is a metaphor in such contexts for power and sovereignty, expresses the reality that the governance of the universe belongs to Him, and whatever He wills is carried out. Therefore, the sentence "Doer of whatever He intends" (فَعَّالٌ لِما يُرِيدُ) is actually a consequence of this absolute sovereignty. Collectively, it demonstrates His power over the matter of Resurrection, the reviving of the dead after death, and the retribution and punishment of tyrants and torturers.

"Majid" (مَجِيد) is derived from "majd," meaning the expansiveness of generosity, nobility, and majesty. This is an attribute specific to Allah and is rarely used for others. [^1] These five attributes have a clear internal consistency, for being "Ghafur" and "Wadud" is beneficial only when the person possesses "power," and His generosity is vast and His blessings endless, so that

[^1]: It should be noted that "Majid" in the above verse, according to the famous recitation, is in the nominative case (marfu') and is an attribute of Allah, not in the genitive case (majrur) as an attribute of the Throne.

He may do whatever He intends, with nothing obstructing His work, no one having the power to oppose Him, and no weakness, languor, doubt, or cancellation occurring in His will.

Ayahs

هَلْ أَتَىٰكَ حَدِيثُ ٱلْجُنُودِ (١٧)
Did you receive the story of the hosts (17)
فِرْعَوْنَ وَثَمُودَ (١٨)
of Pharaoh and Thamud? (18)
بَلِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فِى تَكْذِيبٍ (١٩)
Indeed the faithless dwell in denial, (19)
وَٱللَّهُ مِن وَرَآئِهِم مُّحِيطٌۢ (٢٠)
and Allah besieges them from all around. (20)
بَلْ هُوَ قُرْءَانٌ مَّجِيدٌ (٢١)
Indeed it is a glorious Quran, (21)
فِى لَوْحٍ مَّحْفُوظٍۭ (٢٢)
in a preserved tablet. (22)

Commentary

Have You Seen What Allah Did to the Armies of Pharaoh and Thamud?

The preceding verses expressed the absolute power of Allah and His undisputed sovereignty, as well as a threat to the disbelievers and torturers. To demonstrate that these threats are practical and not merely words or slogans, the verses under discussion address the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: "Has the story of the hosts reached you?" (هَلْ أَتاكَ حَدِيثُ الْجُنُودِ).

These were the vast armies that lined up against the Divine Prophets and rose to fight, imagining they could display their strength against the power of Allah.

Then, referring to two clear examples—one from ancient times and the other from a more recent era—it adds: "Of Pharaoh and Thamud?" (فِرْعَوْنَ وَ ثَمُودَ).

These were the ones, some of whom brought the East and West of the world under their dominion, while others carved into the hearts of mountains and quarried massive stones to build grand houses and palaces, such that no one dared to oppose them.

However, Allah crushed the first group with "water" and the second group with "wind"—both of which are means of human life and are considered among the most subtle and delicate of elements! The waves of the Nile swallowed Pharaoh and his army, and the cold, crushing wind lifted the people of Thamud like blades of straw, eventually casting their lifeless bodies upon the surface of the earth.

This was so the Arab polytheists would know they could achieve nothing. When Allah took such vengeance upon those massive and powerful armies, the situation of those who are weaker and more helpless than them is clear—though the weak and the strong are equal before His power. The selection of the people of "Pharaoh" and "Thamud" from among all previous nations as two examples of rebellious peoples is because both were at the peak of power; one belongs to the distant past (the people of Thamud) and the other to a more recent past (the people of Pharaoh). Furthermore, the Arab nations were familiar with their names and were generally aware of their history.

In the next verse, it adds: "But those who disbelieve persist in denial" (بَلِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي تَكْذِيبٍ).

It is not that the signs of truth are hidden or concealed from anyone; rather, it is stubbornness and enmity that prevent some from finding the path and walking the way of truth.

The use of the term "Bal" (Nay/But), which is used for "Idrab" (moving from one point to another), seems to indicate that this group of polytheists is even worse and more stubborn than the people of Pharaoh and Thamud; they are constantly engaged in denying and rejecting the Quran, utilizing every means for this purpose.

But they must know that "Allah encompasses them from behind" and all are within the grasp of His power (وَ اللَّهُ مِنْ وَرائِهِمْ مُحِيطٌ).

If Allah grants them respite, it is not due to inability or weakness, and if He does not punish them immediately, it is not because they are outside the realm of His power.

The expression "from behind them" (مِنْ وَرائِهِمْ) indicates that they are within the grip of Divine power from every direction, and Allah encompasses them from all sides; therefore, it is impossible for them to escape the grasp of His justice and punishment.

There is also the possibility that this refers to Allah's encompassing knowledge of their actions in all aspects, such that no word, deed, or intention of theirs remains hidden from Him.

In the next verse, it adds: Their insistence on denying the Quran and attributing it to sorcery, soothsaying, or poetry is futile; "Nay, it is a glorious Quran" (بَلْ هُوَ قُرْآنٌ مَجِيدٌ).

"In a Guarded Tablet" (فِي لَوْحٍ مَحْفُوظٍ). It is a word recorded in the Guarded Tablet, where the hands of the unworthy, the devils, and the soothsayers can never reach it, and it remains immune to any kind of change, alteration, addition, or subtraction. Therefore, if they attribute false labels to you and call you a poet, a sorcerer, a soothsayer, or a madman, never be grieved; your foundation is firm, your path is clear, and your Supporter is powerful and capable.

"Majid" (Glorious), as we have said, is derived from the root "Majd," meaning vastness in honor and majesty. This meaning is perfectly applicable to the Quran because its content is grand and vast, and its meanings are lofty and rich—both in the realm of knowledge and beliefs, and in ethics, exhortations, laws, and traditions.

"Lawh" (with a short 'a' sound on the 'L') means a wide surface upon which something is written.

In any case, what is meant here is the "surface" upon which the Glorious Quran is recorded and preserved, but not a surface like the tablets common among us. Rather, in a commentary by Ibn Abbas, it is stated that the "Guarded Tablet" has a length equal to the distance between the earth and the sky, and a width equal to the distance between the West and the East! It is here that it appears the Guarded Tablet is the very "Surface of Allah's Knowledge," which encompasses the East and West of the universe and is protected and preserved from any change or distortion.

Yes, the Quran originates from the infinite knowledge of the Truth; it is neither the product of human thought nor the inspiration of devils, and its content bears witness to this claim.

This is likely the same thing that is sometimes referred to in the Glorious Quran as "Kitabun Mubin" (a Clear Book) and sometimes as "Umm al-Kitab" (the Mother of the Book), as we read in Surah Ar-Ra'd, verse 39: "Allah eliminates what He wills and confirms [what He wills], and with Him is the Mother of the Book" (يَمْحُوا اللَّهُ ما يَشاءُ وَ يُثْبِتُ وَ عِنْدَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ).

And in Surah Al-An'am, verse 59: "And there is no wet or dry [thing] but that it is in a clear record" (وَ لا رَطْبٍ وَ لا يابِسٍ إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ).

Additionally, it should be noted that the expression "Guarded Tablet" (al-Lawh al-Mahfuz) appears in the Glorious Quran only in this instance.

O Allah! Make us more acquainted with the reality of this great Heavenly Book of Yours. O Lord! On that day when the righteous believers attain the "Great Success" and the criminal disbelievers reach the "Punishment of the Burning Fire," place us under the wing of Your protection.

O God! You are the All-Forgiving, the All-Loving, the Most Merciful, and the Compassionate; deal with us as these Names and Attributes of Yours require, not as our deeds deserve.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds.

End of Surah Al-Buruj.
